memories.content:
Prioritize sustained performance and real-world thermal behavior over peak marketing specs when selecting an external drive for active video editing.

----- ORIGINAL SOURCE -----
user said: Yeah, this is basically the right breakdown. The USB-C vs actual speed point is the one people miss all the time.

Main thing I’d keep in mind: for active editing, I’d rather have a solid SSD with real sustained performance than chase the biggest spec number on the box. Especially if I’m throwing 4K or long-form footage at it. HDDs are fine for archive, but not something I’d want as the main working drive unless I had to.

The caveat about thermal throttling is important too. I’ve seen drives look great on paper and then fall off once they heat up. That’s the kind of thing that matters more than marketing copy.

If you want, I can help you make this into a tighter buy/no-buy checklist for Mac or Windows specifically.

User prioritizes practical, immediately usable workflows with clear operational templates. Seeks a concrete folder structure with explicit naming that survives client notes, a reusable revision log, a simple note tracker to avoid cross-channel note chasing, and a built-in review split (internal → client). Values active verification and multi-layered backups but wants the folder structure to be actionable today. Prefers a unified revision log/changelog over two separate systems that can drift. Finds the folder tree, status tags, and handoff checklist solid and ready for immediate use. Now explicitly focused on tightening recurring post-work processes to ensure smoother handoffs. User has adopted a hard stop rule: defer any item not affecting opening, relinking, picture lock, audio sync, or export settings. User prefers a consistent, boring numeric naming scheme (e.g., 01_Working, 02_Review) to prevent team misuse and maintain clarity. User now wants portfolio copy to sound lived-in, grounded in daily reality — short, plain-sentence drafts (1–2 sentences max), one small placeholder like [specialty], and avoids salesy or copywriter-y phrasing. User prefers a 5-minute end-of-day checklist with hard-stop triggers and explicit backup rules for day-to-day use. User prefers a 2-minute version as the primary template and will make the 'risky stretch' line more explicit later. User now wants internal updates to be concise, useful, and direct — name the issue clearly without over-explaining. User explicitly wants updates to sound accountable and specific: explicitly state the delay, give a short reason, and specify a concrete next update time. User rejects generic phrasing like 'I’ll keep you posted' and wants clear ownership of delays. User is actively working to turn messy notes into actionable, followable formats by breaking them into discrete items with clear due times. User prefers to order tasks by the actual tackling order, not by just rewriting them cleanly. User is now particularly focused on real-world drive performance and thermal behavior in video editing workflows. User agrees with plain, internal wording that avoids over-cooking and extra framing.

----- ORIGINAL SOURCE -----
user said: Yeah, this is basically the right shape. But I’d tighten it a bit more so it doesn’t turn into another half-cleaned mess.

What I’d do:
- make one backup first, then freeze the current project state
- build a single tracker for the actual mismatch points, not a big general audit sheet
- separate **confirmed / unconfirmed / client-changed** into hard buckets
- don’t rename folders until the contents are verified against the tracker
- keep a short change log so the next handoff doesn’t depend on memory

The part I’d push harder on is the client shifting scope. That needs its own column or section. Otherwise you end up mixing technical cleanup with approval drift, and that’s where people get lost.

If you want to make this useful for real, I’d want either:
1. a bare-bones cleanup checklist I can use while digging through the project, or
2. a client update that says what’s been stabilized, what changed, and what still needs confirmation.

Also, if you’ve got a lot of version drift, I’d probably add a naming convention for exports and notes before touching anything else. I’ve had projects go sideways just because the latest file was called some random temp name and nobody wanted to admit which one was current.
user said: Yeah, closer. I’d still want it a little more operational though — less general, more like something I can actually work from without second-guessing it.

What I’d tighten:
- make the tracker columns explicit
- include a separate column for **source of truth** / where each item came from
- add a **decision needed** column so unresolved stuff doesn’t just sit there
- keep the change log short enough that I’ll actually maintain it

And I’d probably separate the project into two passes:
1. **stabilize the current state** — backup, freeze, identify current approved version
2. **reconcile the drift** — notes, folder labels, client changes, export naming

That keeps the cleanup from bleeding into the content decisions.

If you’re going to write the checklist, I’d want it in a format I can use while I’m in the project — like a short numbered sequence, not a big explanation. Same with the client note: plain, factual, no drama.

So yeah — the structure is right. I’d just push it one step further into something that can survive an actual messy handoff.
user said: Yeah, that’s usable. The tracker columns are the part that makes it real instead of just sounding organized.

I’d probably only tighten one thing: put **source of truth** and **decision needed** near the front of the sheet, not buried. That’s where the mess usually shows up first.

So the working order I’d actually use is:
1. backup
2. freeze current state
3. identify the approved version
4. build the tracker with explicit mismatch points only
5. sort everything into confirmed / unconfirmed / client-changed
6. reconcile folder labels and notes against the tracker
7. standardize exports last
8. keep the change log short and current

That’s close enough to something I’d actually open while I’m in the project. If you want, the next useful thing would be the one-page checklist or the plain client note.
user said: Yeah, this is the right order. Clean enough to use in-session, and it separates the three buckets without wandering off into theory.

The only thing I’d add is: if the relink was from an archive or a transcode set, check **source duration and timecode first** before you burn time on the sequence. I’ve had stuff look fine until one clip was the same filename but not the same actual media, and that’s where the timeline starts doing dumb things.

So yeah — duplicate first, new sequence test, then one suspect clip, then sequence elements, then media interpretation/version stuff, then cache/app weirdness. That’s usable.
user said: I’m about to hand off a finished edit and want to make sure I’m not missing anything obvious before I send it. Can you help me build a quick last-pass checklist for picture, sound, and exports that’s realistic for a small team and not too bloated?
user said: Yeah, this is the right shape, but it’s still a little generic.

I need the actual decision tree for the three-way mess — like, if the client note is ambiguous but the export is failing, what do I do first, second, third. And if the export fix is likely to eat the whole night, what’s the cutoff where you stop and send a clean status instead of forcing it.

Basically: give me the hard call rules, not just the process.
user said: Yeah, closer. Still need the actual branching logic for the ugly cases, not just the general rule.

What I want is something like:
- if the note is ambiguous and the export is a known-setting problem, do X
- if the note is ambiguous and the export is a blind troubleshoot, do Y
- if the note is clear but would require reopening picture, do Z
- if you’ve burned 20 minutes and have no clean path, then what exactly gets sent in the status

And I want the cutoff stated in real terms — like how much time has to be left for fix + export + QC before you stop. Not just “enough time.”

Also, if there’s a version I can ship that’s technically safe but leaves the note unresolved, I want that spelled out too. The hard rule, basically: what gets prioritized when two things are both bad, and when do you call it instead of gambling on a late-night fix.
user said: Yeah, that’s the right lane. But I’d tighten the order a bit.

I’d check:

1. **Isolated playback of the source clip**
   - Not just relinked or in-sequence. Open the actual media.
   - If it stutters there, stop chasing the timeline.

2. **Sequence/frame-rate mismatch first**
   - That’s one of the most common “looks fine until export” problems.
   - Especially if the error is weird and not clearly tied to one effect.

3. **The exact stutter point in the timeline**
   - One clip? One transition? One nested sequence?
   - If it’s only local, that narrows it fast.

4. **Effects, speed changes, stabilization, noise reduction**
   - Anything computationally heavy or time-remapping related.
   - I’ve had stuff that played marginally okay in timeline but broke on export because of a single effect stack.

5. **Codec / cache / render files**
   - If playback is stuttering but source media is clean, sometimes it’s just bad cache or a nasty render file.
   - Clear preview/cache and test again before rebuilding the whole thing.

6. **Export settings against sequence settings**
   - Wrong frame rate, field order, weird audio settings, or a preset that doesn’t match the timeline.

The one thing I’d add: **don’t assume the export error and playback stutter have the same cause**. Sometimes they do, sometimes not. I’ve had a sequence stutter because of cached preview garbage, while export failed because of one bad transition two minutes later. Annoying, but not rare.

If you want the fastest triage path, I’d go:
**source clip → sequence settings → problem section → disable effects → clear cache → export test**.

That usually tells you pretty quickly whether you’re dealing with media, timeline structure, or render/export weirdness.
user said: Yeah, that’s pretty much the shape of it. The export split is the useful part.

The one thing I’d keep in front of me is: **tiny-range export vs full export** tells you a lot. If a 5–10 second range fails, I’m thinking sequence settings, codec mismatch, or a bad element right there. If the tiny range passes but the full export dies, then I’m looking for something later in the timeline — nested sequence, buried transition, a corrupted frame, something ugly that only gets hit on the full pass.

And yeah, cache first if source is clean and preview is the only thing acting up. I’ve wasted enough time on garbage render files to know better now.

If I were tightening it for actual use, I’d keep it to:
- source media
- cache/preview if playback only is bad
- sequence settings
- exact bad section
- effects / speed changes
- export a short range vs full export

That’s the part that matters. The rest is just noise.
user said: I’m helping someone prep a short video for both online use and a local TV slot, and I want to make sure the delivery specs won’t bite us later. Can you help me sanity-check what usually needs to be different between those two versions and what I should flag before export?
user said: Pretty solid, but I’d want a bit more specificity on the TV side. “Local TV slot” can mean totally different spec sheets depending on the station, and that’s where stuff bites you later.

What I’d flag before export, in practice:
- exact station delivery spec, not generic broadcast talk
- frame rate and field order, if they care
- audio loudness target, true peak, and whether they want stereo or mono
- legal/safe title area for graphics
- whether they want captions, slate, bars, or tone
- codec wrapper and bit depth, because some stations are picky about that more than people expect
- any forced black/slate duration at head or tail

Also, for the web version, I’d keep an eye on whether the encode is being made from the master or from a compressed intermediate. That’s one of those quiet problems that shows up as mushy detail or weird audio after upload.

If you want to make this actually useful on a real job, I’d ask for a tighter two-column checklist with:
1. online export defaults
2. broadcast export defaults
3. final QC pass items for both

Because right now it’s good, just a little generic.
user said: Yeah, this is the right shape. Still a little generic on the broadcast side, though.

What I’d want added is the stuff that actually changes the export decision:
- whether the station wants **progressive or interlaced** delivery
- exact **audio spec**: LUFS/LKFS target, true peak ceiling, and if they want **-23, -24, or something station-specific**
- whether they require **mono fold-down** or true stereo
- **caption format** if it’s not just baked-in video captions
- any **slate/bar/tone** length and placement, especially if they’re strict about head/tail
- **legal safe vs action safe** if graphics are close to the edges
- whether they accept **ProRes 422 vs LT vs DNxHR**, because some stations do care more than they should

And on the web side, I’d also note:
- export from the **cleanest master** you have, not a re-compressed web file
- watch for **audio normalization** differences between NLE export and platform upload
- if there are captions, make sure they’re either properly embedded or delivered in the format the platform actually reads

So yeah — I’d want the checklist to read less like general delivery advice and more like:
1. confirm station spec sheet
2. lock frame rate / field order / audio spec
3. verify safe areas and graphics
4. confirm slate/bars/captions requirements
5. export from master, then QC both versions separately

If you want, I can help tighten it into a real export checklist with the actual QC items under each version.
user said: Yeah, that’s closer. Still one thing I’d want baked in: **don’t assume “station spec sheet” is enough unless someone has actually read the whole thing line by line**. The gotchas are usually in the weeds — embedded vs sidecar captions, whether they want **48 kHz audio**, exact channel layout, and whether they reject files that are technically right but wrapped wrong.

I’d also split the TV side into two buckets:
- **hard specs**: frame rate, interlace/progressive, loudness, peak, codec, audio layout
- **delivery extras**: slate, bars/tone, black, captions, naming convention, file transfer method

Because that’s where jobs get derailed — not the creative part, the paperwork part.

If you want, tighten it one more step into a **pre-export checklist** and a **final QC checklist**. That’s usually the version I’d actually trust on a real job.
user said: Yeah, that’s the version I’d actually trust.

The split into **hard specs** vs **delivery extras** is the useful part, and the 48 kHz / channel layout / wrapper stuff is exactly where people get burned. Also good call on not treating the station sheet like gospel unless somebody has read the whole thing. That’s usually where the nonsense is hiding.

I’d use this as-is on a real job. The only thing I’d maybe add, if I were being picky, is a note to verify whether the station wants **embedded captions vs sidecar** in whatever file wrapper they’re accepting — but you already covered that well enough.

So yeah. Solid.
user said: I need a simple way to clean up a growing pile of project files before it turns into a mess. Can you help me set up a folder and naming approach that makes it easier to find the right version later and keep exports straight?
user said: Yeah, this is basically the right shape. Clean and not overcooked.

What I’d want next is a versioning rule that’s a little tighter, because the `v03_APPROVED / v04_FOR_REVIEW` thing can still get fuzzy once exports start multiplying. Like:
- one naming pattern for **working project files**
- one for **review exports**
- one for **final delivery masters**
- and a rule for what counts as the current file

Also would help to see how you’d handle duplicates from different export specs — H.264, ProRes, maybe audio-only, captions, that kind of thing. That’s where these systems usually fall apart if it’s not explicit.
user said: Yeah, that’s closer. The role tag is the part that keeps it from turning into soup.

I’d still tighten one thing: **current file should be the highest approved version within that role/spec, not just whatever got exported last**. Otherwise somebody drops a fresh review export and now “current” means different things depending on who’s looking.

I’d do it like this:

### Working
`ClientX_wip_v01.prproj`
`ClientX_wip_v02.prproj`
- Only one active working project. If you branch, that branch gets a suffix.

### Review exports
`ClientX_rev_v03_H264.mp4`
`ClientX_rev_v03_ProRes.mov`
- Same cut, same version, different spec.
- If the edit changes, bump to `v04` across all review exports.

### Final masters
`ClientX_fin_v04_ProRes.mov`
`ClientX_fin_v04_H264.mp4`
- Final should only happen after approval.
- If you revise after final, don’t overwrite it. New version, new master.

### Audio / captions
`ClientX_aud_v04_WAV.wav`
`ClientX_cap_v04_SRT.srt`
- These should match the same approved cut version as the picture master.

## My rule for “current”
- **Current working** = latest `wip` file
- **Current review** = latest exported version for that exact cut
- **Current final** = latest approved `fin` file
- Older files stay, but they’re archived by version, not hidden or renamed

## Simple duplicate rule
If it’s the **same cut**:
- keep the same version number
- change only the spec/format

If the **content changes**:
- bump the version number on everything that belongs to that cut

So:
- `v03_H264` and `v03_ProRes` = same edit
- `v04_H264` and `v04_ProRes` = revised edit

## One thing I’d add
Use a tiny status suffix only if needed:
- `DRAFT`
- `REVIEW`
- `APPROVED`
- `DELIVERED`

But I wouldn’t bake status into every filename unless you actually need it. Too much text starts making filenames ugly fast.

If you want, I can give you a **single exact template** next — like one line you can just adopt and a mini example set for a real project.
user said: Yeah. That’s usable.

The only thing I’d probably tweak is making the status optional, not part of the default name. Keep the base clean:

`ClientX_role_v##_spec.ext`

and only add a status tag if there’s a real reason:

`ClientX_rev_v03_H264_REVIEW.mp4`

But honestly, the rule set itself is solid:
- same cut = same version
- different export spec = same version, different spec
- actual edit change = bump version across all matching outputs
- current file = highest approved version for that role/spec

That’s the part that matters. Otherwise people start treating whatever was last touched as “current,” and that’s where the mess starts.

If I were using this in an edit suite, I’d probably keep one extra note file in the project root too, just a plain text changelog with the approved version and delivery targets. Nothing fancy. Helps when you come back to it after a week and the folder has 40 files in it.

So yeah — this is basically the copy-paste standard I’d want.
user said: I’m trying to cut a short promo piece and I’m not sure whether it should lean more on fast momentum or let a few moments breathe. Can you help me think through what usually makes that kind of edit feel clear and engaging without dragging?
user said: Yeah, basically. But it depends what the promo is actually selling.

If it’s a straight hype piece, I’d push the momentum harder. If it’s brand-forward or story-heavy, you need a couple holds or it just turns into noise. Fast cuts are fine, but only if the viewer can still track the idea.

What usually keeps it from dragging:
- clear opening in the first couple seconds
- one idea per beat
- a few longer frames on the best shot, best line, or reveal
- not cutting *just* because you can

I’d think in terms of pace changes, not one constant speed. A run of quick cuts, then a breath, then back up. That contrast is what makes the edit feel intentional.

If you want, I can give you a rough beat structure for 15s, 30s, or 60s — but I’d need to know what kind of promo it is.

- backup / recovery: if a drive or project breaks, what’s the fallback and how fast can I get back

The big thing for me is the handoff between stages. That’s where misses tend to pile up. If the brief is loose at the start, or the versioning gets sloppy midstream, you end up paying for it at export time.

So yeah, useful direction, but I’d rather build this around a tighter intake-to-delivery chain than a general checklist. If you want to keep going, lay it out as a practical sequence: what to lock first, what to check before picture lock, and what to verify right before delivery.

- picture lock: no more structural changes after this unless it’s a real correction
- QC: separate passes, not one vague final look
- recovery: backup and relink tested before the deadline is breathing down your neck

user said: I’ve got a deliverable tonight that’s already getting messy: one file is missing, the timeline needs a couple of fixes, and the clock is not being kind. Can you help me think through the smartest order to attack it so I can still get something solid out the door?

original_turns:
user said: Yeah, this is basically the right shape. But I’d tighten it a bit more so it doesn’t turn into another half-cleaned mess.

What I’d do:
- make one backup first, then freeze the current project state
- build a single tracker for the actual mismatch points, not a big general audit sheet
- separate **confirmed / unconfirmed / client-changed** into hard buckets
- don’t rename folders until the contents are verified against the tracker
- keep a short change log so the next handoff doesn’t depend on memory

The part I’d push harder on is the client shifting scope. That needs its own column or section. Otherwise you end up mixing technical cleanup with approval drift, and that’s where people get lost.

If you want to make this useful for real, I’d want either:
1. a bare-bones cleanup checklist I can use while digging through the project, or
2. a client update that says what’s been stabilized, what changed, and what still needs confirmation.

Also, if you’ve got a lot of version drift, I’d probably add a naming convention for exports and notes before touching anything else. I’ve had projects go sideways just because the latest file was called some random temp name and nobody wanted to admit which one was current.

user said: Yeah, closer. I’d still want it a little more operational though — less general, more like something I can actually work from without second-guessing it.

What I’d tighten:
- make the tracker columns explicit
- include a separate column for **source of truth** / where each item came from
- add a **decision needed** column so unresolved stuff doesn’t just sit there
- keep the change log short enough that I’ll actually maintain it

And I’d probably separate the project into two passes:
1. **stabilize the current state** — backup, freeze, identify current approved version
2. **reconcile the drift** — notes, folder labels, client changes, export naming

That keeps the cleanup from bleeding into the content decisions.

If you’re going to write the checklist, I’d want it in a format I can use while I’m in the project — like a short numbered sequence, not a big explanation. Same with the client note: plain, factual, no drama.

So yeah — the structure is right. I’d just push it one step further into something that can survive an actual messy handoff.

user said: Yeah, that’s usable. The tracker columns are the part that makes it real instead of just sounding organized.

I’d probably only tighten one thing: put **source of truth** and **decision needed** near the front of the sheet, not buried. That’s where the mess usually shows up first.

So the working order I’d actually use is:
1. backup
2. freeze current state
3. identify the approved version
4. build the tracker with explicit mismatch points only
5. sort everything into confirmed / unconfirmed / client-changed
6. reconcile folder labels and notes against the tracker
7. standardize exports last
8. keep the change log short and current

That’s close enough to something I’d actually open while I’m in the project. If you want, the next useful thing would be the one-page checklist or the plain client note.

user said: Yeah, this is the right order. Clean enough to use in-session, and it separates the three buckets without wandering off into theory.

The only thing I’d add is: if the relink was from an archive or a transcode set, check **source duration and timecode first** before you burn time on the sequence. I’ve had stuff look fine until one clip was the same filename but not the same actual media, and that’s where the timeline starts doing dumb things.

So yeah — duplicate first, new sequence test, then one suspect clip, then sequence elements, then media interpretation/version stuff, then cache/app weirdness. That’s usable.

- if the note is ambiguous and the export is a blind troubleshoot, do Y
- if the note is clear but would require reopening picture, do Z

user said: Yeah, that’s the right lane. But I’d tighten the order a bit.

I’d check:

1. **Isolated playback of the source clip**
   - Not just relinked or in-sequence. Open the actual media.
   - If it stutters there, stop chasing the timeline.

2. **Sequence/frame-rate mismatch first**
   - That’s one of the most common “looks fine until export” problems.
   - Especially if the error is weird and not clearly tied to one effect.

3. **The exact stutter point in the timeline**
   - One clip? One transition? One nested sequence?
   - If it’s only local, that narrows it fast.

4. **Effects, speed changes, stabilization, noise reduction**
   - Anything computationally heavy or time-remapping related.
   - I’ve had stuff that played marginally okay in timeline but broke on export because of a single effect stack.

5. **Codec / cache / render files**
   - If playback is stuttering but source media is clean, sometimes it’s just bad cache or a nasty render file.
   - Clear preview/cache and test again before rebuilding the whole thing.

6. **Export settings against sequence settings**
   - Wrong frame rate, field order, weird audio settings, or a preset that doesn’t match the timeline.

The one thing I’d add: **don’t assume the export error and playback stutter have the same cause**. Sometimes they do, sometimes not. I’ve had a sequence stutter because of cached preview garbage, while export failed because of one bad transition two minutes later. Annoying, but not rare.

If you want the fastest triage path, I’d go:
**source clip → sequence settings → problem section → disable effects → clear cache → export test**.

That usually tells you pretty quickly whether you’re dealing with media, timeline structure, or render/export weirdness.

user said: Yeah, that’s pretty much the shape of it. The export split is the useful part.

The one thing I’d keep in front of me is: **tiny-range export vs full export** tells you a lot. If a 5–10 second range fails, I’m thinking sequence settings, codec mismatch, or a bad element right there. If the tiny range passes but the full export dies, then I’m looking for something later in the timeline — nested sequence, buried transition, a corrupted frame, something ugly that only gets hit on the full pass.

And yeah, cache first if source is clean and preview is the only thing acting up. I’ve wasted enough time on garbage render files to know better now.

If I were tightening it for actual use, I’d keep it to:
- source media
- cache/preview if playback only is bad
- sequence settings
- exact bad section
- effects / speed changes
- export a short range vs full export

That’s the part that matters. The rest is just noise.

user said: I’m helping someone prep a short video for both online use and a local TV slot, and I want to make sure the delivery specs won’t bite us later. Can you help me sanity-check what usually needs to be different between those two versions and what I should flag before export?

user said: Pretty solid, but I’d want a bit more specificity on the TV side. “Local TV slot” can mean totally different spec sheets depending on the station, and that’s where stuff bites you later.

What I’d flag before export, in practice:
- exact station delivery spec, not generic broadcast talk
- frame rate and field order, if they care
- audio loudness target, true peak, and whether they want stereo or mono
- legal/safe title area for graphics
- whether they want captions, slate, bars, or tone
- codec wrapper and bit depth, because some stations are picky about that more than people expect
- any forced black/slate duration at head or tail

Also, for the web version, I’d keep an eye on whether the encode is being made from the master or from a compressed intermediate. That’s one of those quiet problems that shows up as mushy detail or weird audio after upload.

If you want to make this actually useful on a real job, I’d ask for a tighter two-column checklist with:
1. online export defaults
2. broadcast export defaults
3. final QC pass items for both

Because right now it’s good, just a little generic.

user said: Yeah, this is the right shape. Still a little generic on the broadcast side, though.

What I’d want added is the stuff that actually changes the export decision:
- whether the station wants **progressive or interlaced** delivery
- exact **audio spec**: LUFS/LKFS target, true peak ceiling, and if they want **-23, -24, or something station-specific**
- whether they require **mono fold-down** or true stereo
- **caption format** if it’s not just baked-in video captions
- any **slate/bar/tone** length and placement, especially if they’re strict about head/tail
- **legal safe vs action safe** if graphics are close to the edges
- whether they accept **ProRes 422 vs LT vs DNxHR**, because some stations do care more than they should

And on the web side, I’d also note:
- export from the **cleanest master** you have, not a re-compressed web file
- watch for **audio normalization** differences between NLE export and platform upload
- if there are captions, make sure they’re either properly embedded or delivered in the format the platform actually reads

So yeah — I’d want the checklist to read less like general delivery advice and more like:
1. confirm station spec sheet
2. lock frame rate / field order / audio spec
3. verify safe areas and graphics
4. confirm slate/bars/captions requirements
5. export from master, then QC both versions separately

If you want, I can help tighten it into a real export checklist with the actual QC items under each version.

user said: Yeah, that’s closer. The role tag is the part that keeps it from turning into soup.

I’d still tighten one thing: **current file should be the highest approved version within that role/spec, not just whatever got exported last**. Otherwise somebody drops a fresh review export and now “current” means different things depending on who’s looking.

I’d do it like this:

### Working
`ClientX_wip_v01.prproj`
`ClientX_wip_v02.prproj`
- Only one active working project. If you branch, that branch gets a suffix.

### Review exports
`ClientX_rev_v03_H264.mp4`
`ClientX_rev_v03_ProRes.mov`
- Same cut, same version, different spec.
- If the edit changes, bump to `v04` across all review exports.

### Final masters
`ClientX_fin_v04_ProRes.mov`
`ClientX_fin_v04_H264.mp4`
- Final should only happen after approval.
- If you revise after final, don’t overwrite it. New version, new master.

### Audio / captions
`ClientX_aud_v04_WAV.wav`
`ClientX_cap_v04_SRT.srt`
- These should match the same approved cut version as the picture master.

## My rule for “current”
- **Current working** = latest `wip` file
- **Current review** = latest exported version for that exact cut
- **Current final** = latest approved `fin` file
- Older files stay, but they’re archived by version, not hidden or renamed

## Simple duplicate rule
If it’s the **same cut**:
- keep the same version number
- change only the spec/format

If the **content changes**:
- bump the version number on everything that belongs to that cut

So:
- `v03_H264` and `v03_ProRes` = same edit
- `v04_H264` and `v04_ProRes` = revised edit

## One thing I’d add
Use a tiny status suffix only if needed:
- `DRAFT`
- `REVIEW`
- `APPROVED`
- `DELIVERED`

But I wouldn’t bake status into every filename unless you actually need it. Too much text starts making filenames ugly fast.

If you want, I can give you a **single exact template** next — like one line you can just adopt and a mini example set for a real project.

user said: I’m trying to cut a short promo piece and I’m not sure whether it should lean more on fast momentum or let a few moments breathe. Can you help me think through what usually makes that kind of edit feel clear and engaging without dragging?

user said: Yeah, basically. But it depends what the promo is actually selling.

If it’s a straight hype piece, I’d push the momentum harder. If it’s brand-forward or story-heavy, you need a couple holds or it just turns into noise. Fast cuts are fine, but only if the viewer can still track the idea.

What usually keeps it from dragging:
- clear opening in the first couple seconds
- one idea per beat
- a few longer frames on the best shot, best line, or reveal
- not cutting *just* because you can

I’d think in terms of pace changes, not one constant speed. A run of quick cuts, then a breath, then back up. That contrast is what makes the edit feel intentional.

If you want, I can give you a rough beat structure for 15s, 30s, or 60s — but I’d need to know what kind of promo it is.

3. fix the scene 4 lower-third typo
4. check interview audio levels

7. verify final file names against the delivery sheet

That’s closer to how I’d actually work it tonight. If you want, I can tighten it into a literal checkbox list with no extra explanation.
user said: Yeah, that works. Clean and usable.

user said: Yeah, this is basically the right breakdown. The USB-C vs actual speed point is the one people miss all the time.

Main thing I’d keep in mind: for active editing, I’d rather have a solid SSD with real sustained performance than chase the biggest spec number on the box. Especially if I’m throwing 4K or long-form footage at it. HDDs are fine for archive, but not something I’d want as the main working drive unless I had to.

The caveat about thermal throttling is important too. I’ve seen drives look great on paper and then fall off once they heat up. That’s the kind of thing that matters more than marketing copy.

If you want, I can help you make this into a tighter buy/no-buy checklist for Mac or Windows specifically.

Seeks a concrete folder structure with explicit naming that survives client notes, a reusable revision log, a simple note tracker to avoid cross-channel note chasing, and a built-in review split (internal → client). Values active verification and multi-layered backups but wants the folder structure to be actionable today. Prefers a unified revision log/changelog over two separate systems that can drift. Finds the folder tree, status tags, and handoff checklist solid and ready for immediate use. Now explicitly focused on tightening recurring post-work processes to ensure smoother handoffs. User has adopted a hard stop rule: defer any item not affecting opening, relinking, picture lock, audio sync, or export settings. User prefers a consistent, boring numeric naming scheme (e.g., 01_Working, 02_Review) to prevent team misuse and maintain clarity. User now wants portfolio copy to sound lived-in, grounded in daily reality — short, plain-sentence drafts (1–2 sentences max), one small placeholder like [specialty], and avoids salesy or copywriter-y phrasing. user said: Pretty good, but it’s still a little broad. I’d want a version that’s more lock-specific — stuff like handles, captions/subs if they’re in play, split edits that need a second look, and making sure the project has the exact approved sequence/version. Also maybe a line for verifying export notes against the latest client round, because that’s where things drift. Keep it short, though. I don’t need a monster doc.
user said: Yeah, this is the right shape. Short, lock-specific, not bloated. The approved sequence/version line and the export notes check are the bits I was after. Good enough to use.
user said: I’ve got a post project that got messy fast: a few folders are mislabeled, some media notes don’t line up, and the client keeps shifting what they want included. Can you help me figure out a sane way to untangle it and get it back on track without making the next handoff worse?
user said: Yeah, this is basically the right shape. But I’d tighten it a bit more so it doesn’t turn into another half-cleaned mess.

What I’d do:
- make one backup first, then freeze the current project state
- build a single tracker for the actual mismatch points, not a big general audit sheet
- separate **confirmed / unconfirmed / client-changed** into hard buckets
- don’t rename folders until the contents are verified against the tracker
- keep a short change log so the next handoff doesn’t depend on memory

The part I’d push harder on is the client shifting scope. That needs its own column or section. Otherwise you end up mixing technical cleanup with approval drift, and that’s where people get lost.

If you want to make this useful for real, I’d want either:
1. a bare-bones cleanup checklist I can use while digging through the project, or
2. a client update that says what’s been stabilized, what changed, and what still needs confirmation.

Also, if you’ve got a lot of version drift, I’d probably add a naming convention for exports and notes before touching anything else. I’ve had projects go sideways just because the latest file was called some random temp name and nobody wanted to admit which one was current.
user said: Yeah, closer. I’d still want it a little more operational though — less general, more like something I can actually work from without second-guessing it.

What I’d tighten:
- make the tracker columns explicit
- include a separate column for **source of truth** / where each item came from
- add a **decision needed** column so unresolved stuff doesn’t just sit there
- keep the change log short enough that I’ll actually maintain it

And I’d probably separate the project into two passes:
1. **stabilize the current state** — backup, freeze, identify current approved version
2. **reconcile the drift** — notes, folder labels, client changes, export naming

That keeps the cleanup from bleeding into the content decisions.

If you’re going to write the checklist, I’d want it in a format I can use while I’m in the project — like a short numbered sequence, not a big explanation. Same with the client note: plain, factual, no drama.

So yeah — the structure is right. I’d just push it one step further into something that can survive an actual messy handoff.
user said: Yeah, that’s usable. The tracker columns are the part that makes it real instead of just sounding organized.

I’d probably only tighten one thing: put **source of truth** and **decision needed** near the front of the sheet, not buried. That’s where the mess usually shows up first.

So the working order I’d actually use is:
1. backup
2. freeze current state
3. identify the approved version
4. build the tracker with explicit mismatch points only
5. sort everything into confirmed / unconfirmed / client-changed
6. reconcile folder labels and notes against the tracker
7. standardize exports last
8. keep the change log short and current

That’s close enough to something I’d actually open while I’m in the project. If you want, the next useful thing would be the one-page checklist or the plain client note.
user said: I’ve got a post session where the timeline started acting weird after a relink, and I’m trying to figure out the fastest way to narrow down whether it’s a media issue, a sequence issue, or something else. Can you help me lay out a clean troubleshooting order so I don’t waste time chasing the wrong thing?
user said: Yeah, this is the right order. Clean enough to use in-session, and it separates the three buckets without wandering off into theory.

The only thing I’d add is: if the relink was from an archive or a transcode set, check **source duration and timecode first** before you burn time on the sequence. I’ve had stuff look fine until one clip was the same filename but not the same actual media, and that’s where the timeline starts doing dumb things.

So yeah — duplicate first, new sequence test, then one suspect clip, then sequence elements, then media interpretation/version stuff, then cache/app weirdness. That’s usable.
user said: I’m about to hand off a finished edit and want to make sure I’m not missing anything obvious before I send it. Can you help me build a quick last-pass checklist for picture, sound, and exports that’s realistic for a small team and not too bloated?
user said: Pretty good, but I’d tighten it a bit more for actual use. This is still slightly bloated for a small team — a few items overlap.

I’d want one pass that separates:
- picture QC
- audio QC
- export / delivery QC
- handoff / archive

And I’d add a couple things that matter in real life:
- burn-in / slate / timecode if the delivery needs it
- aspect ratio / safe area / captions language if applicable
- final runtime against spec
- check the actual file name, not just the version number in your head
- verify checksum / upload succeeded if it’s going to Frame.io, Aspera, Drive, whatever

Also, “handles” is only relevant in some workflows, so maybe don’t keep that in the main list unless you’re doing turnovers.

If you want, make it even leaner — like a 12-item checklist I can actually use before I send something out.
user said: Yeah, that’s the one. Clean enough to actually use, and it stops repeating itself.

The only tiny tweak I’d make is maybe keep “burn-in/slate/timecode” on the same line as the picture checks like you did, since that’s only for some deliveries. Otherwise it’s solid.

The copy/paste doc version would be useful too, if you’ve got it.
user said: Yeah, this is the right idea. But I’d want it tighter, more operational — like actual blocks, not just categories.

I need something I can use tonight:
- how to split the next 2–3 days by hour or half-day
- where to put the revision review vs actual cutting
- when to stop touching picture and just QC
- a buffer for export/upload, because that’s always where something stupid shows up

Also, if you can build in a point where I’m forced to freeze the cut and not keep noodling, that’d help. That’s usually where I burn time for no real gain.
user said: Yeah, closer. But I need it more like a real working schedule, not just phases.

Give me something with actual blocks, like:
- 7:00–9:30 revision triage
- 9:30–12:30 cut the must-fix notes
- 12:30–1:00 quick review / break
- etc.

And be explicit about the freeze point. I want a hard line where picture is locked, not “clean enough to review.” Something like: after this block, only QC-safe fixes, no timing nudges, no little trims, no smoothing unless it breaks something.

Also build in a real buffer for export/upload — I usually need more than 1 hour because something dumb always happens with render time, relink, or upload. So maybe 2–3 hours minimum if the file is heavy.

If you can, map it for 2 days and 3 days separately. I’d rather see the exact tradeoff than a generic template.
user said: Yeah — this is usable. The hard freeze line is the part I needed, and the 2-day vs 3-day tradeoff is clear.

The only thing I’d probably tweak for my own use is moving the freeze a little earlier on Day 2 if the cut is already close, just so export doesn’t get squeezed. But the structure itself is right. The 2–3 hour export/upload buffer is the realistic part. That’s usually where the stupid stuff happens.
user said: Yeah, this is the right shape, but it’s still a little generic.

I need the actual decision tree for the three-way mess — like, if the client note is ambiguous but the export is failing, what do I do first, second, third. And if the export fix is likely to eat the whole night, what’s the cutoff where you stop and send a clean status instead of forcing it.

Basically: give me the hard call rules, not just the process.
user said: Yeah, closer. Still need the actual branching logic for the ugly cases, not just the general rule.

What I want is something like:
- if the note is ambiguous and the export is a known-setting problem, do X
- if the note is ambiguous and the export is a blind troubleshoot, do Y
- if the note is clear but would require reopening picture, do Z
- if you’ve burned 20 minutes and have no clean path, then what exactly gets sent in the status

And I want the cutoff stated in real terms — like how much time has to be left for fix + export + QC before you stop. Not just “enough time.”

Also, if there’s a version I can ship that’s technically safe but leaves the note unresolved, I want that spelled out too. The hard rule, basically: what gets prioritized when two things are both bad, and when do you call it instead of gambling on a late-night fix.
user said: Yeah. That’s the first version that actually feels usable.

The 45-minute cutoff is the part I needed spelled out. And the safe-ship rule — last approved version, note logged unresolved — is the right call. I’d rather send the technically clean file and eat the note later than start guessing at 11:40 and make a worse problem.

The branch order makes sense too:
- ambiguous note + known fix = fix export first
- ambiguous note + blind troubleshoot = 20 minutes, then stop
- clear note + reopening picture = only if the full chain still fits
- export fixed but note unresolved = ship the approved version
- 20 minutes burned, no path = status, not gambling

That’s the decision tree I needed.
user said: I’m trying to clean up a short edit that’s been stuttering in playback and throwing a weird export error, and I want to make sure I’m not chasing the wrong fix. Can you help me think through the most likely causes and the order you’d check them in?
user said: Yeah, that’s the right lane. But I’d tighten the order a bit.

I’d check:

1. **Isolated playback of the source clip**
   - Not just relinked or in-sequence. Open the actual media.
   - If it stutters there, stop chasing the timeline.

2. **Sequence/frame-rate mismatch first**
   - That’s one of the most common “looks fine until export” problems.
   - Especially if the error is weird and not clearly tied to one effect.

3. **The exact stutter point in the timeline**
   - One clip? One transition? One nested sequence?
   - If it’s only local, that narrows it fast.

4. **Effects, speed changes, stabilization, noise reduction**
   - Anything computationally heavy or time-remapping related.
   - I’ve had stuff that played marginally okay in timeline but broke on export because of a single effect stack.

5. **Codec / cache / render files**
   - If playback is stuttering but source media is clean, sometimes it’s just bad cache or a nasty render file.
   - Clear preview/cache and test again before rebuilding the whole thing.

6. **Export settings against sequence settings**
   - Wrong frame rate, field order, weird audio settings, or a preset that doesn’t match the timeline.

The one thing I’d add: **don’t assume the export error and playback stutter have the same cause**. Sometimes they do, sometimes not. I’ve had a sequence stutter because of cached preview garbage, while export failed because of one bad transition two minutes later. Annoying, but not rare.

If you want the fastest triage path, I’d go:
**source clip → sequence settings → problem section → disable effects → clear cache → export test**.

That usually tells you pretty quickly whether you’re dealing with media, timeline structure, or render/export weirdness.
user said: Yeah, basically. I’d just want the **exact export error behavior** pinned down a little more, because that changes the order for me.

If the error happens on **every export**, even a tiny range, I’d suspect sequence settings / codec mismatch / a bad element in the problem section first.
If it only happens on the **full export**, then I’d look harder at something later in the timeline — buried transition, nested sequence, corrupted frame, whatever.

Also, if the stutter is only in **preview** and the source file plays clean, I’d clear cache before I start ripping apart effects. Been burned by that more than once. Garbage render files can make you chase the wrong clip for an hour.

So I’d keep the order, but I’d split it like this:
- source media
- sequence settings
- exact bad section
- cache/render files
- effects / speed changes
- export test on a short range

If you want, I’d go one step further and make it a **decision tree** instead of a list. That’s usually faster once you’re in the edit and the clock’s running.
user said: Yeah, that’s pretty much the shape of it. The export split is the useful part.

The one thing I’d keep in front of me is: **tiny-range export vs full export** tells you a lot. If a 5–10 second range fails, I’m thinking sequence settings, codec mismatch, or a bad element right there. If the tiny range passes but the full export dies, then I’m looking for something later in the timeline — nested sequence, buried transition, a corrupted frame, something ugly that only gets hit on the full pass.

And yeah, cache first if source is clean and preview is the only thing acting up. I’ve wasted enough time on garbage render files to know better now.

If I were tightening it for actual use, I’d keep it to:
- source media
- cache/preview if playback only is bad
- sequence settings
- exact bad section
- effects / speed changes
- export a short range vs full export

That’s the part that matters. The rest is just noise.
user said: I’m helping someone prep a short video for both online use and a local TV slot, and I want to make sure the delivery specs won’t bite us later. Can you help me sanity-check what usually needs to be different between those two versions and what I should flag before export?
user said: Pretty solid, but I’d want a bit more specificity on the TV side. “Local TV slot” can mean totally different spec sheets depending on the station, and that’s where stuff bites you later.

What I’d flag before export, in practice:
- exact station delivery spec, not generic broadcast talk
- frame rate and field order, if they care
- audio loudness target, true peak, and whether they want stereo or mono
- legal/safe title area for graphics
- whether they want captions, slate, bars, or tone
- codec wrapper and bit depth, because some stations are picky about that more than people expect
- any forced black/slate duration at head or tail

Also, for the web version, I’d keep an eye on whether the encode is being made from the master or from a compressed intermediate. That’s one of those quiet problems that shows up as mushy detail or weird audio after upload.

If you want to make this actually useful on a real job, I’d ask for a tighter two-column checklist with:
1. online export defaults
2. broadcast export defaults
3. final QC pass items for both

Because right now it’s good, just a little generic.
user said: Yeah, this is the right shape. Still a little generic on the broadcast side, though.

What I’d want added is the stuff that actually changes the export decision:
- whether the station wants **progressive or interlaced** delivery
- exact **audio spec**: LUFS/LKFS target, true peak ceiling, and if they want **-23, -24, or something station-specific**
- whether they require **mono fold-down** or true stereo
- **caption format** if it’s not just baked-in video captions
- any **slate/bar/tone** length and placement, especially if they’re strict about head/tail
- **legal safe vs action safe** if graphics are close to the edges
- whether they accept **ProRes 422 vs LT vs DNxHR**, because some stations do care more than they should

And on the web side, I’d also note:
- export from the **cleanest master** you have, not a re-compressed web file
- watch for **audio normalization** differences between NLE export and platform upload
- if there are captions, make sure they’re either properly embedded or delivered in the format the platform actually reads

So yeah — I’d want the checklist to read less like general delivery advice and more like:
1. confirm station spec sheet
2. lock frame rate / field order / audio spec
3. verify safe areas and graphics
4. confirm slate/bars/captions requirements
5. export from master, then QC both versions separately

If you want, I can help tighten it into a real export checklist with the actual QC items under each version.
user said: Yeah, that’s closer. Still one thing I’d want baked in: **don’t assume “station spec sheet” is enough unless someone has actually read the whole thing line by line**. The gotchas are usually in the weeds — embedded vs sidecar captions, whether they want **48 kHz audio**, exact channel layout, and whether they reject files that are technically right but wrapped wrong.

I’d also split the TV side into two buckets:
- **hard specs**: frame rate, interlace/progressive, loudness, peak, codec, audio layout
- **delivery extras**: slate, bars/tone, black, captions, naming convention, file transfer method

Because that’s where jobs get derailed — not the creative part, the paperwork part.

If you want, tighten it one more step into a **pre-export checklist** and a **final QC checklist**. That’s usually the version I’d actually trust on a real job.
user said: Yeah, that’s the version I’d actually trust.

The split into **hard specs** vs **delivery extras** is the useful part, and the 48 kHz / channel layout / wrapper stuff is exactly where people get burned. Also good call on not treating the station sheet like gospel unless somebody has read the whole thing. That’s usually where the nonsense is hiding.

I’d use this as-is on a real job. The only thing I’d maybe add, if I were being picky, is a note to verify whether the station wants **embedded captions vs sidecar** in whatever file wrapper they’re accepting — but you already covered that well enough.

So yeah. Solid.
user said: I need a simple way to clean up a growing pile of project files before it turns into a mess. Can you help me set up a folder and naming approach that makes it easier to find the right version later and keep exports straight?
user said: Yeah, this is basically the right shape. Clean and not overcooked.

What I’d want next is a versioning rule that’s a little tighter, because the `v03_APPROVED / v04_FOR_REVIEW` thing can still get fuzzy once exports start multiplying. Like:
- one naming pattern for **working project files**
- one for **review exports**
- one for **final delivery masters**
- and a rule for what counts as the current file

Also would help to see how you’d handle duplicates from different export specs — H.264, ProRes, maybe audio-only, captions, that kind of thing. That’s where these systems usually fall apart if it’s not explicit.
user said: Yeah, that’s closer. The role tag is the part that keeps it from turning into soup.

I’d still tighten one thing: **current file should be the highest approved version within that role/spec, not just whatever got exported last**. Otherwise somebody drops a fresh review export and now “current” means different things depending on who’s looking.

I’d do it like this:

### Working
`ClientX_wip_v01.prproj`
`ClientX_wip_v02.prproj`
- Only one active working project. If you branch, that branch gets a suffix.

### Review exports
`ClientX_rev_v03_H264.mp4`
`ClientX_rev_v03_ProRes.mov`
- Same cut, same version, different spec.
- If the edit changes, bump to `v04` across all review exports.

### Final masters
`ClientX_fin_v04_ProRes.mov`
`ClientX_fin_v04_H264.mp4`
- Final should only happen after approval.
- If you revise after final, don’t overwrite it. New version, new master.

### Audio / captions
`ClientX_aud_v04_WAV.wav`
`ClientX_cap_v04_SRT.srt`
- These should match the same approved cut version as the picture master.

## My rule for “current”
- **Current working** = latest `wip` file
- **Current review** = latest exported version for that exact cut
- **Current final** = latest approved `fin` file
- Older files stay, but they’re archived by version, not hidden or renamed

## Simple duplicate rule
If it’s the **same cut**:
- keep the same version number
- change only the spec/format

If the **content changes**:
- bump the version number on everything that belongs to that cut

So:
- `v03_H264` and `v03_ProRes` = same edit
- `v04_H264` and `v04_ProRes` = revised edit

## One thing I’d add
Use a tiny status suffix only if needed:
- `DRAFT`
- `REVIEW`
- `APPROVED`
- `DELIVERED`

But I wouldn’t bake status into every filename unless you actually need it. Too much text starts making filenames ugly fast.

If you want, I can give you a **single exact template** next — like one line you can just adopt and a mini example set for a real project.
user said: Yeah. That’s usable.

The only thing I’d probably tweak is making the status optional, not part of the default name. Keep the base clean:

`ClientX_role_v##_spec.ext`

and only add a status tag if there’s a real reason:

`ClientX_rev_v03_H264_REVIEW.mp4`

But honestly, the rule set itself is solid:
- same cut = same version
- different export spec = same version, different spec
- actual edit change = bump version across all matching outputs
- current file = highest approved version for that role/spec

That’s the part that matters. Otherwise people start treating whatever was last touched as “current,” and that’s where the mess starts.

If I were using this in an edit suite, I’d probably keep one extra note file in the project root too, just a plain text changelog with the approved version and delivery targets. Nothing fancy. Helps when you come back to it after a week and the folder has 40 files in it.

So yeah — this is basically the copy-paste standard I’d want.
user said: I’m trying to cut a short promo piece and I’m not sure whether it should lean more on fast momentum or let a few moments breathe. Can you help me think through what usually makes that kind of edit feel clear and engaging without dragging?
user said: Yeah, basically. But it depends what the promo is actually selling.

If it’s a straight hype piece, I’d push the momentum harder. If it’s brand-forward or story-heavy, you need a couple holds or it just turns into noise. Fast cuts are fine, but only if the viewer can still track the idea.

What usually keeps it from dragging:
- clear opening in the first couple seconds
- one idea per beat
- a few longer frames on the best shot, best line, or reveal
- not cutting *just* because you can

I’d think in terms of pace changes, not one constant speed. A run of quick cuts, then a breath, then back up. That contrast is what makes the edit feel intentional.

If you want, I can give you a rough beat structure for 15s, 30s, or 60s — but I’d need to know what kind of promo it is.
user said: Yeah, that’s the right shape of it. Pace should serve the message, not just the cut rhythm.

The only thing I’d add is the practical version: if you’re unsure, cut it a little tighter first, then give air back only to the moments that actually need to land. That keeps you from drifting into a slow, self-important promo for no reason.

So basically: hook fast, keep the ideas clean, and use breathing room as emphasis — not as default.
user said: I’m trying to decide whether it’s smarter to buy a new laptop outright or do a monthly payment plan for it, and I don’t want to make a dumb call. Can you help me think through the tradeoffs in plain English and what I should watch for before I pick one?

The only thing I’d add is: before export, do a quick compare against the last approved cut, not just the current timeline. That’s the part that usually catches the quiet stuff — a slipped trim, wrong title card, or some audio change nobody flagged.

Otherwise the sequence is solid.
user said: I’m trying to tighten up my post workflow after a couple of annoying misses, and I want a practical way to stop the same issues from slowing me down again. Can you help me think through what parts of the process are worth revising first so the next round runs cleaner?
user said: Pretty close. But I’d want this aimed a little more at where the actual failure points are in post, not just generic workflow cleanup.

The parts I’d revise first are:
- intake / brief: what exactly is being delivered, to whom, by when, and in what specs
- project setup: folder structure, naming, proxies, archive path, versioning
- change control: what counts as a real revision vs. noise, and who signs off
- QC: separate picture, audio, captions/slate, export settings, and upload verification
- backup / recovery: if a drive or project breaks, what’s the fallback and how fast can I get back

The big thing for me is the handoff between stages. That’s where misses tend to pile up. If the brief is loose at the start, or the versioning gets sloppy midstream, you end up paying for it at export time.

So yeah, useful direction, but I’d rather build this around a tighter intake-to-delivery chain than a general checklist. If you want to keep going, lay it out as a practical sequence: what to lock first, what to check before picture lock, and what to verify right before delivery.
user said: Yeah, that’s the shape. But I’d want it a bit more operational — less “what the stages are,” more “what gets locked so the job doesn’t drift.”

The part that still matters most to me is the handoff discipline:
- intake: lock the brief, specs, due date, approval chain
- setup: mirror the brief in folders, naming, versioning, media paths
- change control: log every real change and make one person the authority on it
- picture lock: no more structural changes after this unless it’s a real correction
- QC: separate passes, not one vague final look
- recovery: backup and relink tested before the deadline is breathing down your neck

If you want to make this actually useful, I’d like it laid out as a **one-page workflow** with:
1. what has to be confirmed at intake,
2. what must be true before picture lock,
3. what gets checked in final delivery.

And maybe call out the common failure points at each handoff — that’s the part I’d actually use.
user said: Yeah — that’s usable. This is much closer to what I meant.

The handoff framing is right, and the failure points are the useful part. Intake / setup / change control / picture lock / QC / recovery is basically the chain I’d want to keep in front of me.

The only thing I’d probably want next is the actual copy-paste checklist version, since that’s what I’d end up using day to day. But as a one-page workflow, this works.

- separate **confirmed / unconfirmed / client-changed** into hard buckets

1. a bare-bones cleanup checklist I can use while digging through the project, or
2. a client update that says what’s been stabilized, what changed, and what still needs confirmation.

Also, if you’ve got a lot of version drift, I’d probably add a naming convention for exports and notes before touching anything else. I’ve had projects go sideways just because the latest file was called some random temp name and nobody wanted to admit which one was current.

user said: I’m trying to clean up a short edit that’s been stuttering in playback and throwing a weird export error, and I want to make sure I’m not chasing the wrong fix. Can you help me think through the most likely causes and the order you’d check them in?

user said: Yeah, that’s the right lane. But I’d tighten the order a bit.

I’d check:

1. **Isolated playback of the source clip**
   - Not just relinked or in-sequence. Open the actual media.
   - If it stutters there, stop chasing the timeline.

2. **Sequence/frame-rate mismatch first**
   - That’s one of the most common “looks fine until export” problems.
   - Especially if the error is weird and not clearly tied to one effect.

3. **The exact stutter point in the timeline**
   - One clip? One transition? One nested sequence?
   - If it’s only local, that narrows it fast.

4. **Effects, speed changes, stabilization, noise reduction**
   - Anything computationally heavy or time-remapping related.
   - I’ve had stuff that played marginally okay in timeline but broke on export because of a single effect stack.

5. **Codec / cache / render files**
   - If playback is stuttering but source media is clean, sometimes it’s just bad cache or a nasty render file.
   - Clear preview/cache and test again before rebuilding the whole thing.

6. **Export settings against sequence settings**
   - Wrong frame rate, field order, weird audio settings, or a preset that doesn’t match the timeline.

The one thing I’d add: **don’t assume the export error and playback stutter have the same cause**. Sometimes they do, sometimes not. I’ve had a sequence stutter because of cached preview garbage, while export failed because of one bad transition two minutes later. Annoying, but not rare.

If you want the fastest triage path, I’d go:
**source clip → sequence settings → problem section → disable effects → clear cache → export test**.

That usually tells you pretty quickly whether you’re dealing with media, timeline structure, or render/export weirdness.

user said: Yeah, basically. I’d just want the **exact export error behavior** pinned down a little more, because that changes the order for me.

If the error happens on **every export**, even a tiny range, I’d suspect sequence settings / codec mismatch / a bad element in the problem section first.
If it only happens on the **full export**, then I’d look harder at something later in the timeline — buried transition, nested sequence, corrupted frame, whatever.

Also, if the stutter is only in **preview** and the source file plays clean, I’d clear cache before I start ripping apart effects. Been burned by that more than once. Garbage render files can make you chase the wrong clip for an hour.

So I’d keep the order, but I’d split it like this:
- source media
- sequence settings
- exact bad section
- cache/render files
- effects / speed changes
- export test on a short range

If you want, I’d go one step further and make it a **decision tree** instead of a list. That’s usually faster once you’re in the edit and the clock’s running.

user said: Yeah, that’s pretty much the shape of it. The export split is the useful part.

The one thing I’d keep in front of me is: **tiny-range export vs full export** tells you a lot. If a 5–10 second range fails, I’m thinking sequence settings, codec mismatch, or a bad element right there. If the tiny range passes but the full export dies, then I’m looking for something later in the timeline — nested sequence, buried transition, a corrupted frame, something ugly that only gets hit on the full pass.

And yeah, cache first if source is clean and preview is the only thing acting up. I’ve wasted enough time on garbage render files to know better now.

If I were tightening it for actual use, I’d keep it to:
- source media
- cache/preview if playback only is bad
- sequence settings
- exact bad section
- effects / speed changes
- export a short range vs full export

That’s the part that matters. The rest is just noise.

user said: I’m helping someone prep a short video for both online use and a local TV slot, and I want to make sure the delivery specs won’t bite us later. Can you help me sanity-check what usually needs to be different between those two versions and what I should flag before export?

user said: Pretty solid, but I’d want a bit more specificity on the TV side. “Local TV slot” can mean totally different spec sheets depending on the station, and that’s where stuff bites you later.

What I’d flag before export, in practice:
- exact station delivery spec, not generic broadcast talk
- frame rate and field order, if they care
- audio loudness target, true peak, and whether they want stereo or mono
- legal/safe title area for graphics
- whether they want captions, slate, bars, or tone
- codec wrapper and bit depth, because some stations are picky about that more than people expect
- any forced black/slate duration at head or tail

Also, for the web version, I’d keep an eye on whether the encode is being made from the master or from a compressed intermediate. That’s one of those quiet problems that shows up as mushy detail or weird audio after upload.

If you want to make this actually useful on a real job, I’d ask for a tighter two-column checklist with:
1. online export defaults
2. broadcast export defaults
3. final QC pass items for both

Because right now it’s good, just a little generic.

What I’d want added is the stuff that actually changes the export decision:
- whether the station wants **progressive or interlaced** delivery
- exact **audio spec**: LUFS/LKFS target, true peak ceiling, and if they want **-23, -24, or something station-specific**
- whether they require **mono fold-down** or true stereo
- **caption format** if it’s not just baked-in video captions
- any **slate/bar/tone** length and placement, especially if they’re strict about head/tail
- **legal safe vs action safe** if graphics are close to the edges
- whether they accept **ProRes 422 vs LT vs DNxHR**, because some stations do care more than they should

And on the web side, I’d also note:
- export from the **cleanest master** you have, not a re-compressed web file
- watch for **audio normalization** differences between NLE export and platform upload
- if there are captions, make sure they’re either properly embedded or delivered in the format the platform actually reads

1. confirm station spec sheet
2. lock frame rate / field order / audio spec
3. verify safe areas and graphics
4. confirm slate/bars/captions requirements
5. export from master, then QC both versions separately

If you want, I can help tighten it into a real export checklist with the actual QC items under each version.

user said: Yeah, this is basically the right shape. Clean and not overcooked.

- one for **review exports**
- one for **final delivery masters**
- and a rule for what counts as the current file

Also would help to see how you’d handle duplicates from different export specs — H.264, ProRes, maybe audio-only, captions, that kind of thing. That’s where these systems usually fall apart if it’s not explicit.

user said: Yeah, that’s closer. The role tag is the part that keeps it from turning into soup.

I’d still tighten one thing: **current file should be the highest approved version within that role/spec, not just whatever got exported last**. Otherwise somebody drops a fresh review export and now “current” means different things depending on who’s looking.

I’d do it like this:

### Working
`ClientX_wip_v01.prproj`
`ClientX_wip_v02.prproj`
- Only one active working project. If you branch, that branch gets a suffix.

### Review exports
`ClientX_rev_v03_H264.mp4`
`ClientX_rev_v03_ProRes.mov`
- Same cut, same version, different spec.
- If the edit changes, bump to `v04` across all review exports.

### Final masters
`ClientX_fin_v04_ProRes.mov`
`ClientX_fin_v04_H264.mp4`
- Final should only happen after approval.
- If you revise after final, don’t overwrite it. New version, new master.

### Audio / captions
`ClientX_aud_v04_WAV.wav`
`ClientX_cap_v04_SRT.srt`
- These should match the same approved cut version as the picture master.

## My rule for “current”
- **Current working** = latest `wip` file
- **Current review** = latest exported version for that exact cut
- **Current final** = latest approved `fin` file
- Older files stay, but they’re archived by version, not hidden or renamed

## Simple duplicate rule
If it’s the **same cut**:
- keep the same version number
- change only the spec/format

If the **content changes**:
- bump the version number on everything that belongs to that cut

So:
- `v03_H264` and `v03_ProRes` = same edit
- `v04_H264` and `v04_ProRes` = revised edit

## One thing I’d add
Use a tiny status suffix only if needed:
- `DRAFT`
- `REVIEW`
- `APPROVED`
- `DELIVERED`

But I wouldn’t bake status into every filename unless you actually need it. Too much text starts making filenames ugly fast.

If you want, I can give you a **single exact template** next — like one line you can just adopt and a mini example set for a real project.

- one idea per beat
- a few longer frames on the best shot, best line, or reveal
- not cutting *just* because you can

- who signs off, and in what order

2. **Source + project housekeeping**
- folder map
- file naming convention
- camera reports / missing media notes
- version labels for sequences and exports
- backup location, if there is one

3. **Revision control**
- what counts as a client note vs. a new request
- where notes live
- current approved version
- what changed since last review

4. **QC pass checklist**
- picture continuity
- titles / graphics spelling
- audio levels and clip issues
- export settings
- caption timing / burn-in vs sidecar, if relevant

5. **Delivery check**
- exported file opens cleanly
- playback checked after upload
- delivered version matches what was approved

The part I’d push hardest is the **“current source of truth”** and the **change log**. That’s where things usually get muddy. I’ve had projects go sideways just because someone was looking at the wrong cut or assumed a note had already been folded in.

If you want, I can help you turn this into a tighter template for post — like a real intake/QC sheet you could keep beside the monitor.

user said: Yeah, this is basically the right breakdown. The USB-C vs actual speed point is the one people miss all the time.

Main thing I’d keep in mind: for active editing, I’d rather have a solid SSD with real sustained performance than chase the biggest spec number on the box. Especially if I’m throwing 4K or long-form footage at it. HDDs are fine for archive, but not something I’d want as the main working drive unless I had to.

The caveat about thermal throttling is important too. I’ve seen drives look great on paper and then fall off once they heat up. That’s the kind of thing that matters more than marketing copy.

If you want, I can help you make this into a tighter buy/no-buy checklist for Mac or Windows specifically.
